Periodic Table Flip Cards

Curious Chemist's Edition

Bigger cards with the full descriptions. Click or tap a card to flip it. The front shows the element's name and full write-up; flip it over to reveal the symbol and figure out exactly where it sits on the periodic table.

Alkali metal
Alkaline earth metal
Transition metal
Post-transition metal
Metalloid
Reactive nonmetal
Halogen
Noble gas
Lanthanide
Actinide
Hydrogen
Reactive nonmetal

Hydrogen is the simplest, lightest, and most abundant chemical element in the universe. At standard conditions, it is a colorless, odorless, and highly flammable diatomic gas. It plays a fundamental role in powering stars through nuclear fusion and is a primary constituent of water and all organic matter.

Z = 1
H
Hydrogen
Helium
Noble gas

Helium is a colorless, odorless, and tasteless noble gas that is the second-lightest element in the universe. Because it is completely chemically inert, it does not easily form compounds with other elements. It is widely known for its exceptionally low boiling point and its modern uses in cryogenics, rocketry, and high-altitude balloons.

Z = 2
He
Helium
Lithium
Alkali metal

Lithium is a soft, silvery-white alkali metal and the least dense of all solid elements. Like all alkali metals, it is highly reactive with water and must be stored in mineral oil to prevent oxidation. It is highly valued today for its critical role in rechargeable lithium-ion batteries and various lightweight metal alloys.

Z = 3
Li
Lithium
Beryllium
Alkaline earth metal

Beryllium is a relatively rare, steel-gray alkaline earth metal that is remarkably lightweight but incredibly rigid. It possesses a high melting point and excellent thermal conductivity, making it highly useful in aerospace components and structural materials. However, its dust is highly toxic to humans and can cause chronic lung disease.

Z = 4
Be
Beryllium
Boron
Metalloid

Boron is a low-abundance metalloid that is commonly found concentrated in water-soluble evaporite mineral deposits like borax. In its crystalline form, it is an extremely hard, brittle, and heat-resistant dark semiconductor. It is widely utilized in the manufacturing of fiberglass, specialized borosilicate glassware, and various agricultural nutrients.

Z = 5
B
Boron
Carbon
Reactive nonmetal

Carbon is a nonmetallic element that serves as the chemical basis for all known organic life due to its unique ability to form stable bonds with many elements. It exists in several distinct allotropes, ranging from the incredibly soft, opaque graphite to the ultra-hard, transparent diamond. Carbon is also a primary component of fossil fuels and plays a major role in regulating the Earth's climate.

Z = 6
C
Carbon
Nitrogen
Reactive nonmetal

Nitrogen is a colorless, odorless gas that makes up roughly 78% of the Earth’s atmosphere as a stable diatomic molecule. It is a critical component of amino acids, proteins, and DNA, making it essential for all living organisms. Industrially, vast quantities of nitrogen are captured via the Haber-Bosch process to manufacture synthetic fertilizers.

Z = 7
N
Nitrogen
Oxygen
Reactive nonmetal

Oxygen is a highly reactive nonmetal and strong oxidizing agent that constitutes about 21% of the Earth's atmosphere. It is indispensable for the respiration of most living organisms and is a core component of water and organic molecules. It also forms the ozone layer (O₃) in the upper atmosphere, which shields the planet from harmful ultraviolet radiation.

Z = 8
O
Oxygen
Fluorine
Halogen

Fluorine is an extremely toxic, pale yellow halogen gas and the most chemically reactive of all elements. It reacts violently with almost all other substances, including glass and noble gases. In everyday applications, trace amounts of its compounds (fluorides) are widely used to prevent dental cavities and in the production of specialized polymers like Teflon.

Z = 9
F
Fluorine
Neon
Noble gas

Neon is a colorless, odorless, and chemically inert noble gas that glows with a distinct reddish-orange light when utilized in a high-voltage electrical discharge tube. Although it is quite rare on Earth, it is the fifth most abundant element in the universe by mass. Its primary commercial applications include vacuum tubes, high-voltage indicators, and iconic advertising signage.

Z = 10
Ne
Neon
Sodium
Alkali metal

Sodium is a soft, silvery-white, and exceptionally reactive alkali metal that reacts violently with water to produce hydrogen gas. Because of this extreme reactivity, it never occurs as a free element in nature and must be extracted from abundant compounds like sodium chloride (common table salt). It is vital for animal biology, serving as an essential electrolyte for nerve signaling and fluid balance.

Z = 11
Na
Sodium
Magnesium
Alkaline earth metal

Magnesium is a shiny gray alkaline earth metal that burns with an intensely bright white light when ignited in air. It is the eighth-most abundant element in the Earth's crust and forms a crucial structural component of chlorophyll in plants. Because of its low density and high structural strength, it is heavily utilized in lightweight structural alloys for automotive and aerospace industries.

Z = 12
Mg
Magnesium
Aluminium
Post-transition metal

Aluminium is a silvery-white, lightweight, and non-magnetic post-transition metal. It is renowned for its low density and its ability to resist corrosion via a protective surface layer of passivation. Despite being the most abundant metal in the Earth's crust, it is rarely found in its pure native state and must be industrially smelted from bauxite ore.

Z = 13
Al
Aluminium
Silicon
Metalloid

Silicon is a hard, brittle, dark gray metalloid with a distinct metallic luster. It is a fundamental semiconductor, making it the bedrock material for modern computer microchips, transistors, and solar cells. As the second-most abundant element in the Earth's crust by mass, it forms the basis of most rocks, clays, sands, and soils.

Z = 14
Si
Silicon
Phosphorus
Reactive nonmetal

Phosphorus is a highly reactive nonmetal that exists in several distinct forms, most notably as volatile, highly toxic white phosphorus and stable, reddish-brown red phosphorus. Because it is highly reactive, it is never found as a free element on Earth and is instead mined from phosphate rocks. It is essential to life, forming the structural backbone of DNA, RNA, and ATP within cells.

Z = 15
P
Phosphorus
Sulfur
Reactive nonmetal

Sulfur is an abundant, multivalent nonmetal that typically forms bright yellow crystalline solids at room temperature. It has a rich history dating back to antiquity, often referred to as 'brimstone,' and burns with a distinctive blue flame that releases a pungent odor. It is a critical component of proteins and is used globally to produce sulfuric acid, a pillar of modern industrial chemistry.

Z = 16
S
Sulfur
Chlorine
Halogen

Chlorine is a pale green, toxic, and pungent halogen gas that is a highly effective oxidizing agent. It is widely used as a disinfectant to sanitize swimming pools and purify drinking water, as well as a bleaching agent in paper manufacturing. In nature, it is most commonly found bound as chloride ions in vast salt deposits and ocean water.

Z = 17
Cl
Chlorine
Argon
Noble gas

Argon is a colorless, odorless noble gas and the third-most abundant gas in the Earth's atmosphere, making up about 0.93% of air. It is entirely chemically inert, providing a highly stable, non-reactive atmosphere for industrial welding, incandescent lighting, and semiconductor fabrication. It is typically isolated on a large scale via the fractional distillation of liquid air.

Z = 18
Ar
Argon
Potassium
Alkali metal

Potassium is a soft, silvery alkali metal that can easily be cut with a knife and reacts rapidly with atmospheric oxygen and moisture. It reacts vigorously with water, generating enough heat to ignite the resulting hydrogen gas with a distinct lilac-colored flame. It is an indispensable dietary nutrient for living organisms, sustaining proper cellular function and heart rhythms.

Z = 19
K
Potassium
Calcium
Alkaline earth metal

Calcium is a reactive, dull-gray alkaline earth metal that forms a protective oxide-nitride layer when exposed to air. It is the fifth-most abundant element in the Earth's crust and serves as a fundamental building block for many sedimentary rocks like limestone, marble, and chalk. Biologically, it is critical for structural strength in animal bones, teeth, and shells.

Z = 20
Ca
Calcium
Scandium
Transition metal

Scandium is a rare, silvery-white transition metal that historically hovered on the fringes of early periodic tables as an predicted gap. It is primarily utilized in specialized aluminum alloys to vastly increase weldability, thermal stability, and structural strength. Its compounds also find niche applications in high-intensity stadium lighting and sports equipment.

Z = 21
Sc
Scandium
Titanium
Transition metal

Titanium is a lustrous, silver-colored transition metal celebrated for its incredible strength-to-weight ratio and its exceptional resistance to corrosion. It is highly resistant to degradation by seawater, aqua regia, and chlorine gas. Because it is highly biocompatible and non-toxic, it is widely utilized for surgical implants, joint replacements, and aerospace engineering.

Z = 22
Ti
Titanium
Vanadium
Transition metal

Vanadium is a hard, silvery-gray, and highly malleable transition metal that rarely occurs as a free element in nature. The majority of industrially produced vanadium is utilized as an additive to create high-strength steel alloys, such as those found in armor plating and crankshafts. It is also famous for forming a wide variety of beautifully colored chemical compounds across multiple oxidation states.

Z = 23
V
Vanadium
Chromium
Transition metal

Chromium is a steely-gray, lustrous, and hard transition metal that takes a high mirror polish. It is the primary additive used to create stainless steel, providing superb resistance to tarnish and corrosion. Its name is derived from the Greek word for color due to the remarkably vibrant and varied colors of its chemical compounds.

Z = 24
Cr
Chromium
Manganese
Transition metal

Manganese is a hard, brittle, silvery transition metal that is commonly found in nature bound alongside iron in various minerals. It is absolutely essential to industrial metallurgy, where it is used to desulfurize and strengthen steel alloys. Biologically, it is a crucial trace nutrient required for the activation of many vital metabolic enzymes.

Z = 25
Mn
Manganese
Iron
Transition metal

Iron is a heavy, lustrous transition metal and the most abundant element on Earth by mass, forming much of the planet's outer and inner core. It has been used by humanity for thousands of years, defining entire eras of technological progress via steel production. Biologically, it is the central atom in hemoglobin, the protein responsible for transporting oxygen through the bloodstreams of vertebrates.

Z = 26
Fe
Iron
Cobalt
Transition metal

Cobalt is a hard, lustrous, ferromagnetic transition metal that is structurally similar to iron and nickel. Historically, its ores were used by ancient civilizations to impart a deep, brilliant blue color to glass, ceramics, and inks. Today, it is a critical component in superalloys for jet engines and in the cathodes of lithium-ion batteries.

Z = 27
Co
Cobalt
Nickel
Transition metal

Nickel is a silvery-white, hard, and malleable transition metal that exhibits a slight golden tinge. It is highly resistant to oxidation and corrosion, making it a valuable material for electroplating, coinage, and high-performance alloys like stainless steel. A significant portion of the Earth's core is believed to be composed of an iron-nickel alloy.

Z = 28
Ni
Nickel
Copper
Transition metal

Copper is a soft, malleable, and highly ductile metal characterized by its distinct reddish-orange metallic luster. It possesses exceptionally high thermal and electrical conductivity, making it the global standard for electrical wiring, electronics, and power transmission. It was one of the first metals ever smelted by humanity, playing a pivotal role in the Bronze Age.

Z = 29
Cu
Copper
Zinc
NOT a Transition Metal

Zinc is a bluish-white, moderately reactive transition metal that is brittle at room temperature but becomes malleable when heated. It is extensively used as a protective coating to galvanize iron and steel against rust and corrosion. It is also an essential trace mineral for human health, playing a core role in immune function, DNA synthesis, and cellular division.

Z = 30
Zn
Zinc
Gallium
Post-transition metal

Gallium is a soft, silvery post-transition metal that is famous for its remarkably low melting point of roughly 29.76°C, allowing it to liquefy when held in a warm human hand. It does not exist as a free metal in nature and is primarily extracted as a trace component from aluminum ores. In modern technology, it is highly critical for semiconductors, LEDs, and laser diodes.

Z = 31
Ga
Gallium
Germanium
Metalloid

Germanium is a hard, brittle, grayish-white metalloid that shares structural similarities with silicon and carbon. Its existence was famously predicted by Dmitri Mendeleev before its actual discovery in a mineral sample. It is an essential material in semiconductor electronics, infrared night-vision optics, and fiber-optic communication networks.

Z = 32
Ge
Germanium
Arsenic
Metalloid

Arsenic is a notorious metalloid that occurs in various allotropes, though its metallic gray form is the most common. Historically infamous as a potent and undetectable poison, it has seen widespread use in pesticides, wood preservatives, and specialized metal alloys. Today, highly purified arsenic is utilized in the electronics industry to manufacture gallium arsenide semiconductors.

Z = 33
As
Arsenic
Selenium
Reactive nonmetal

Selenium is a versatile nonmetal that exhibits photovoltaic and photoconductive properties, meaning its electrical conductivity increases significantly when exposed to light. It is relatively rare in the Earth's crust, typically found as a byproduct of copper refining. It is widely used in the manufacturing of glass, solar panels, photocells, and as a vital dietary trace nutrient.

Z = 34
Se
Selenium
Bromine
Halogen

Bromine is a heavy, volatile, reddish-brown halogen liquid at room temperature that readily evaporates into an intensely pungent, suffocating vapor. It is the only nonmetallic element that remains liquid under standard conditions. It is highly corrosive and toxic, with its extracted compounds being used primarily in flame retardants, water treatment, and specialized pharmaceuticals.

Z = 35
Br
Bromine
Krypton
Noble gas

Krypton is a colorless, odorless, and tasteless noble gas that occurs in trace amounts within the Earth's atmosphere. When insulated inside a gas-discharge tube, it glows with a bright whitish-green light. It is commonly utilized in specialized high-speed photographic flashes, energy-efficient fluorescent lighting, and precise tracking lasers.

Z = 36
Kr
Krypton
Rubidium
Alkali metal

Rubidium is an extremely soft, silvery-white alkali metal that ranks as one of the most reactive elements on the periodic table. It ignites spontaneously when exposed to atmospheric air and reacts violently with water, generating an explosion. Because it is highly sensitive, it is primarily used in specialized research, atomic clocks, and vapor turbines.

Z = 37
Rb
Rubidium
Strontium
Alkaline earth metal

Strontium is a highly chemically reactive, soft, silvery-yellow alkaline earth metal that turns dark when exposed to air. It is well-known for imparting a brilliant, deep crimson-red color to flames, making its compounds a cornerstone ingredient in pyrotechnics and distress flares.

Z = 38
Sr
Strontium
Yttrium
Transition metal

Yttrium is a silvery-metallic transition metal that is chemically similar to the lanthanides and is historically categorized as a rare-earth element. It is heavily utilized as an additive to strengthen alloys and is critical in the manufacturing of synthetic garnets. Its compounds have historically been used to produce the red phosphors in old cathode-ray tube televisions.

Z = 39
Y
Yttrium
Zirconium
Transition metal

Zirconium is a lustrous, greyish-white, and highly corrosion-resistant transition metal. It has a remarkably low absorption rate for neutrons, which makes it exceptionally valuable as a cladding material for nuclear reactor fuel rods. Its naturally occurring oxide form, zirconia, is widely used to create ultra-durable ceramics and affordable diamond stimulants.

Z = 40
Zr
Zirconium
Niobium
Transition metal

Niobium, historically known in some early texts as columbium, is a light grey, crystalline, and highly ductile transition metal. It becomes a powerful superconductor at exceptionally low temperatures, making it invaluable for superconducting magnets in MRI machines and particle accelerators. It is also added to steel to form high-strength structural superalloys.

Z = 41
Nb
Niobium
Molybdenum
Transition metal

Molybdenum is a hard, silvery-grey transition metal that possesses one of the highest melting points of all pure elements. It does not occur as a free metal on Earth and is primarily sourced from the mineral molybdenite. Its exceptional thermal stability makes it a vital alloy agent in high-strength steels, military armor, and electrical contacts.

Z = 42
Mo
Molybdenum
Technetium
Transition metal

Technetium was the first element to be produced artificially rather than discovered in nature, filling a gap on the periodic table that had puzzled chemists for decades. This silvery-gray transition metal has no stable isotopes; all of it is radioactive, though its most useful isotope, technetium-99m, is injected into millions of patients each year as a medical imaging tracer. Small amounts do occur naturally in uranium ores as a product of spontaneous fission, but essentially all of the world's supply is manufactured in nuclear reactors.

Z = 43
Tc
Technetium
Ruthenium
Transition metal

Ruthenium is a rare, hard, silvery-white transition metal found alongside platinum in ores and used to harden platinum and palladium alloys for durable electrical contacts and jewelry. It also serves as a catalyst in the chemical industry and coats hard disk drive platters to boost storage density. Ruthenium is highly resistant to corrosion, and some of its compounds glow vividly under UV light, making them useful in scientific sensors.

Z = 44
Ru
Ruthenium
Rhodium
Transition metal

Rhodium is a rare, extremely reflective, silvery-white transition metal best known for its role in catalytic converters, where it helps break down harmful nitrogen oxide emissions from vehicle exhaust. It is also used to electroplate white gold and platinum jewelry, giving them a bright, tarnish-resistant finish. Rhodium is one of the least reactive metals and among the most expensive precious metals by weight.

Z = 45
Rh
Rhodium
Palladium
Transition metal

Palladium is a soft, silvery-white transition metal prized for its ability to absorb up to 900 times its own volume of hydrogen gas, making it valuable for hydrogen storage and purification. Like its neighbors platinum and rhodium, it is widely used in catalytic converters and electronics. Palladium is also a key material in dentistry, jewelry, and multi-layer ceramic capacitors.

Z = 46
Pd
Palladium
Silver
Transition metal

Silver is a soft, lustrous, white transition metal with the highest electrical and thermal conductivity of any element, making it valuable in electronics, mirrors, and solar panels despite its high cost relative to copper. It has been prized for millennia in coinage, jewelry, and tableware, and its antimicrobial properties have led to its use in wound dressings and water purification. Silver naturally tarnishes when exposed to sulfur compounds in the air.

Z = 47
Ag
Silver
Cadmium
Transition metal

Cadmium is a soft, bluish-white, toxic transition metal historically used to make bright yellow, orange, and red pigments and to coat steel to prevent corrosion. It was also a key ingredient in older nickel-cadmium rechargeable batteries, though its toxicity has driven a shift toward safer alternatives like lithium-ion cells. Cadmium accumulates in the body over time, so exposure is now tightly regulated.

Z = 48
Cd
Cadmium
Indium
Post-transition metal

Indium is a soft, silvery post-transition metal so malleable it can be cut with a knife, and it is famous for making a distinctive high-pitched 'cry' when bent. Its most important modern use is as indium tin oxide, a transparent conductive coating found in touchscreens, flat-panel displays, and solar cells. Indium is rare and mostly recovered as a byproduct of zinc ore processing.

Z = 49
In
Indium
Tin
Post-transition metal

Tin is a silvery, malleable post-transition metal that has been used by humans for thousands of years, most famously alloyed with copper to make bronze. Today it is best known as a protective, corrosion-resistant coating on steel food cans and as the primary metal in electronics solder. Pure tin can undergo 'tin pest,' a structural transformation into a brittle powder at very low temperatures.

Z = 50
Sn
Tin
Antimony
Metalloid

Antimony is a lustrous, brittle metalloid that has been used since antiquity in cosmetics, particularly as the dark eye makeup kohl. Modern uses include flame-retardant compounds, lead-acid battery alloys, and semiconductors. Antimony expands slightly when it solidifies, an unusual property shared with only a few other elements, including water and bismuth.

Z = 51
Sb
Antimony
Tellurium
Metalloid

Tellurium is a rare, brittle, silvery-white metalloid with a faint metallic luster and a notorious side effect: even trace exposure can cause a person's breath to smell of garlic for weeks. It is used to improve the machinability of steel and copper alloys and, increasingly, in cadmium telluride thin-film solar panels. Tellurium is one of the rarest stable elements in Earth's crust.

Z = 52
Te
Tellurium
Iodine
Halogen

Iodine is a lustrous, dark purple-black nonmetal that sublimes directly from solid to a violet vapor when heated. It is essential to human health as a building block of thyroid hormones, and iodine deficiency remains a global public health concern addressed through iodized table salt. Its antiseptic properties have also made it a staple of wound care and water purification for over a century.

Z = 53
I
Iodine
Xenon
Noble gas

Xenon is a rare, colorless, and virtually unreactive noble gas that glows with an intense blue-white light when electrified, a property exploited in high-intensity camera flashes, movie projector bulbs, and some car headlights. It is also used as a propellant in ion thrusters for spacecraft, prized for its high mass and efficiency. Despite being classified as a noble gas, xenon can form a handful of unusual compounds under special conditions.

Z = 54
Xe
Xenon
Cesium
Alkali metal

Cesium (also spelled caesium) is an extremely soft, pale gold alkali metal so reactive that it explodes on contact with water and must be stored under oil or in a vacuum. It is best known for defining the modern second: the vibrations of a cesium-133 atom form the basis of atomic clocks, the most precise timekeeping devices ever built. Cesium compounds are also used in drilling fluids for the oil and gas industry.

Z = 55
Cs
Cesium
Barium
Alkaline earth metal

Barium is a soft, silvery alkaline earth metal that reacts vigorously with air and water, so it is never found free in nature. Its most familiar use is in medical imaging, where patients drink a chalky 'barium meal' or receive a barium enema so that the digestive tract shows up clearly on X-rays. Barium compounds also produce the vivid green color seen in many fireworks.

Z = 56
Ba
Barium
Lanthanum
Lanthanide

Lanthanum is a soft, silvery-white metal and the first member of the lanthanide series, elements often called the rare earths. It is used in high-refractive-index camera and telescope lenses, in nickel-metal-hydride rechargeable batteries, and as a catalyst in petroleum refining. Lanthanum tarnishes quickly in air and reacts readily with water, so it is usually stored away from moisture.

Z = 57
La
Lanthanum
Cerium
Lanthanide

Cerium is the most abundant of the rare-earth lanthanide elements, more common in Earth's crust than copper. It is best known for its use in catalytic converters, self-cleaning ovens, and the flint mechanism of lighters, where a cerium-iron alloy called ferrocerium throws sparks when struck. Cerium's compounds also polish glass to an exceptionally fine finish.

Z = 58
Ce
Cerium
Praseodymium
Lanthanide

Praseodymium is a soft, silvery lanthanide metal that develops a green tarnish when exposed to air. It is combined with neodymium to create some of the strongest permanent magnets available and is used to color glass and ceramics a distinctive yellow-green. Praseodymium also improves the strength of magnesium alloys used in aircraft engines.

Z = 59
Pr
Praseodymium
Neodymium
Lanthanide

Neodymium is a bright, silvery lanthanide metal best known for neodymium magnets, the strongest type of permanent magnet commercially produced and a key component in headphones, hard drives, and electric vehicle motors. It is also used to tint glass a violet-blue shade prized by artists and to make protective welding goggles that filter intense yellow light. Neodymium tarnishes quickly and is typically stored away from air.

Z = 60
Nd
Neodymium
Promethium
Lanthanide

Promethium is one of only two elements lighter than bismuth with no stable isotopes, meaning every atom of it is radioactive and it exists on Earth only in trace amounts or in laboratories. It is used in small quantities to make luminous paint and in nuclear-powered batteries for equipment that must run for years without maintenance, such as spacecraft instruments. Promethium's rarity kept it undiscovered until 1945, decades after most other elements.

Z = 61
Pm
Promethium
Samarium
Lanthanide

Samarium is a moderately hard, silvery lanthanide metal best known for samarium-cobalt magnets, which resist demagnetization at high temperatures better than most other magnetic materials. It is also used in nuclear reactor control rods, where it absorbs excess neutrons, and in certain cancer treatments as a radioactive isotope that targets bone tumors. Samarium slowly oxidizes in air, forming a dull gray coating.

Z = 62
Sm
Samarium
Europium
Lanthanide

Europium is a soft, silvery lanthanide metal that is the most reactive of the rare earths, tarnishing almost instantly in air. It is prized for producing the red and blue phosphors used in television screens, energy-efficient lighting, and euro banknotes, where it provides an anti-counterfeiting glow under UV light. Europium compounds are also used as tracers in forensic and biomedical research.

Z = 63
Eu
Europium
Gadolinium
Lanthanide

Gadolinium is a silvery, malleable lanthanide metal notable for its strong magnetic and neutron-absorbing properties. Its most familiar use is as a contrast agent injected before MRI scans, where it sharpens the image of soft tissues and blood vessels. Gadolinium is also used in nuclear reactor shielding and in specialized memory devices.

Z = 64
Gd
Gadolinium
Terbium
Lanthanide

Terbium is a silvery-white, malleable lanthanide metal used mainly to produce green phosphors in energy-efficient fluorescent lamps and television screens. It is also combined with other elements to make magnetostrictive alloys, which change shape in a magnetic field and are used in sonar and precision actuators. Terbium is one of the rarer and more expensive rare-earth elements.

Z = 65
Tb
Terbium
Dysprosium
Lanthanide

Dysprosium is a soft, silvery lanthanide metal essential to modern permanent magnets, where even small additions dramatically improve their resistance to losing magnetism at high temperatures, such as inside electric vehicle motors and wind turbine generators. It also absorbs neutrons effectively, giving it a role in nuclear reactor control rods. Dysprosium is highly reactive with water and must be stored carefully.

Z = 66
Dy
Dysprosium
Holmium
Lanthanide

Holmium is a relatively soft, silvery lanthanide metal that has the highest magnetic strength of any naturally occurring element, making it useful for creating the strongest artificial magnetic fields in laboratory research. It is also used in some solid-state lasers for medical and dental procedures. Holmium oxide is used as a calibration standard because of its extremely sharp optical absorption lines.

Z = 67
Ho
Holmium
Erbium
Lanthanide

Erbium is a silvery-white lanthanide metal best known for its use in fiber-optic communication cables, where erbium-doped segments amplify light signals over long distances, forming the backbone of the modern internet. It is also used in certain surgical and dermatological lasers and gives glass and ceramics a pink tint. Erbium is relatively stable in air compared to other lanthanides.

Z = 68
Er
Erbium
Thulium
Lanthanide

Thulium is the least abundant of the naturally occurring lanthanides and one of the rarest rare-earth elements, though still far more common than gold. It is used in portable X-ray devices, since certain thulium isotopes emit radiation useful for medical and industrial imaging without needing a full-size X-ray machine. Thulium's high cost and scarcity limit it to specialized applications.

Z = 69
Tm
Thulium
Ytterbium
Lanthanide

Ytterbium is a soft, silvery lanthanide metal notable for its use in advanced atomic clocks that are even more precise than the cesium clocks that currently define the second. It is also added to stainless steel to improve its strength and used as a radiation source in portable X-ray units. Ytterbium is unusual among lanthanides for having stable +2 as well as +3 compounds.

Z = 70
Yb
Ytterbium
Lutetium
Lanthanide

Lutetium is the heaviest, hardest, and densest of the lanthanide elements, and it is sometimes classified as the first element of the third transition metal series instead. It is used in PET scan detectors, where lutetium-based crystals convert incoming radiation into detectable light flashes, and as a catalyst in petroleum refining. Lutetium is one of the rarest and most expensive rare earths to extract and purify.

Z = 71
Lu
Lutetium
Hafnium
Transition metal

Hafnium is a lustrous, silvery transition metal that is chemically similar to its lighter neighbor zirconium, making the two notoriously difficult to separate. Its excellent ability to absorb neutrons makes it valuable in nuclear reactor control rods, while its extremely high melting point suits it to jet engine superalloys and advanced semiconductor components. Hafnium was one of the last stable elements to be discovered, in 1923.

Z = 72
Hf
Hafnium
Tantalum
Transition metal

Tantalum is a hard, blue-gray transition metal renowned for its resistance to corrosion by acids, which makes it valuable for chemical processing equipment and surgical implants that must withstand the body's fluids for decades. Its most widespread modern use, however, is in tiny tantalum capacitors found inside smartphones, laptops, and other compact electronics. Tantalum is often sourced from conflict-sensitive regions, making its supply chain a subject of ongoing scrutiny.

Z = 73
Ta
Tantalum
Tungsten
Transition metal

Tungsten has the highest melting point of any metal, over 3,400 degrees Celsius, which for over a century made it the standard filament material in incandescent light bulbs. Its extreme hardness and density also make it valuable in cutting tools, armor-piercing ammunition, and radiation shielding. Tungsten's name comes from Swedish for 'heavy stone,' while its chemical symbol, W, derives from wolframite, the mineral it was first isolated from.

Z = 74
W
Tungsten
Rhenium
Transition metal

Rhenium is one of the rarest elements in Earth's crust, never found as its own ore but recovered as a byproduct of molybdenum and copper mining. It has an extremely high melting point and excellent resistance to wear, making it essential in nickel-based superalloys used for jet engine turbine blades. Rhenium was one of the last stable elements to be discovered, isolated in 1925 and named after the Rhine river.

Z = 75
Re
Rhenium
Osmium
Transition metal

Osmium is the densest naturally occurring element, nearly twice as dense as lead, and it has a distinctive bluish-gray tint compared to the silvery shine of most other metals. It is extremely hard and brittle, and alloyed with other platinum-group metals it is used to tip fountain pens, phonograph needles, and electrical contacts that must resist wear for decades. Powdered osmium reacts with air to form a toxic vapor, so it is normally handled as a solid alloy.

Z = 76
Os
Osmium
Iridium
Transition metal

Iridium is one of the most corrosion-resistant metals known, unaffected by acids, water, or air even at high temperatures, which is why the original international standard meter and kilogram bars were made from platinum-iridium alloys. It is also found in unusually high concentrations in the geological layer marking the asteroid impact linked to the extinction of the dinosaurs. Modern uses include spark plug electrodes and crucibles for growing high-purity crystals.

Z = 77
Ir
Iridium
Platinum
Transition metal

Platinum is a dense, precious, silvery-white metal so unreactive that it does not tarnish or corrode even when exposed to air and most acids for centuries. Beyond its long history in fine jewelry, platinum is a critical catalyst in catalytic converters, fuel cells, and many industrial chemical processes. It is rarer than gold, and most of the world's supply comes from just two countries.

Z = 78
Pt
Platinum
Gold
Transition metal

Gold is a dense, soft, brilliantly yellow precious metal so resistant to corrosion that ancient gold artifacts still gleam today exactly as they did thousands of years ago. Beyond jewelry and currency, its excellent electrical conductivity and resistance to tarnishing make it essential in the connectors of smartphones, computers, and spacecraft electronics. Gold is so malleable that a single ounce can be hammered into a sheet covering roughly 300 square feet.

Z = 79
Au
Gold
Mercury
Transition metal

Mercury is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature, a striking silvery fluid once used widely in thermometers, barometers, and dental fillings. It is highly toxic, especially in its vapor and organic forms, which has led to its phase-out from most consumer products worldwide. Ancient civilizations were fascinated by mercury's shimmering, ever-moving appearance, and it gave rise to the term 'mercurial' for something quick and unpredictable.

Z = 80
Hg
Mercury
Thallium
Post-transition metal

Thallium is a soft, gray post-transition metal historically nicknamed the 'poisoner's poison' because it is odorless, tasteless, and highly toxic, once used in rat and insect poisons before those uses were banned. Modern applications are far narrower, including specialized glass and electronics, as well as a radioactive isotope once used in heart imaging. Thallium tarnishes quickly in moist air, developing a bluish-gray oxide layer.

Z = 81
Tl
Thallium
Lead
Post-transition metal

Lead is a dense, soft, bluish-gray metal that has been used by humans for over 6,000 years, once common in water pipes, paint, and gasoline additives before its serious health risks, especially to children's neurological development, became widely understood. It remains important today in lead-acid car batteries and radiation shielding, where its density blocks X-rays and gamma rays effectively. Lead's Latin name, plumbum, is the origin of the word 'plumbing.'

Z = 82
Pb
Lead
Bismuth
Post-transition metal

Bismuth is a brittle, silvery-pink post-transition metal famous for the strikingly colorful, iridescent oxide layer that forms on its surface when it cools slowly after melting, making it popular for decorative crystals. It is one of the few metals that expands when it solidifies, and it is the active ingredient in the stomach-soothing medicine bismuth subsalicylate. Unlike its neighbor lead, bismuth is only very weakly radioactive and is considered relatively safe to handle.

Z = 83
Bi
Bismuth
Polonium
Metalloid

Polonium is an intensely radioactive metalloid discovered by Marie and Pierre Curie in 1898 and named after Marie Curie's native Poland. It releases so much energy through radioactive decay that a small sample can glow with self-generated heat and light, a property that has been explored for compact power sources in spacecraft. Polonium is also notorious for its use as a poison in several high-profile assassination cases.

Z = 84
Po
Polonium
Astatine
Halogen

Astatine is the rarest naturally occurring element on Earth, with only about one ounce estimated to exist in the planet's crust at any given time due to its extreme radioactivity and rapid decay. It is a halogen, chemically related to iodine, bromine, and chlorine, though it decays so quickly that its physical properties have barely been observed directly. Astatine is being studied for use in targeted cancer radiotherapy.

Z = 85
At
Astatine
Radon
Noble gas

Radon is a colorless, odorless, radioactive noble gas that forms naturally from the decay of uranium and thorium in rocks and soil, and it can seep into basements and lower floors of buildings. It is the second-leading cause of lung cancer after smoking, which has led to widespread home radon testing and mitigation efforts. Despite this danger, radon was once mistakenly marketed as a health tonic in the early twentieth century.

Z = 86
Rn
Radon
Francium
Alkali metal

Francium is an extremely rare, highly radioactive alkali metal, with less than an ounce estimated to exist naturally on Earth at any time because it decays within minutes of forming. It is the second-least electronegative element and would theoretically be the most reactive alkali metal, but it is far too scarce and short-lived to observe directly or put to practical use. Francium was the last element discovered in nature rather than synthesized, found in 1939.

Z = 87
Fr
Francium
Radium
Alkaline earth metal

Radium is a highly radioactive alkaline earth metal that glows faintly blue in the dark, a property that led to its infamous early-20th-century use in luminous paint for watch dials, clocks, and instrument panels. The workers who painted these dials, known as the 'Radium Girls,' suffered severe health effects that helped establish modern workplace safety and radiation protection standards. Radium was discovered by Marie and Pierre Curie in 1898, alongside polonium.

Z = 88
Ra
Radium
Actinium
Actinide

Actinium is a soft, silvery-white radioactive metal that glows with a pale blue light in the dark due to the intense radiation it emits, which excites the surrounding air. It is the first element of the actinide series and gives that series its name. Actinium is exceedingly rare in nature and is mostly produced artificially in nuclear reactors for research and limited medical applications.

Z = 89
Ac
Actinium
Thorium
Actinide

Thorium is a naturally occurring, weakly radioactive metal that is roughly three times more abundant in Earth's crust than uranium, making it a subject of ongoing research as an alternative nuclear fuel that produces less long-lived radioactive waste. It was once used to make the mantles of gas lanterns glow brightly. Thorium was named after Thor, the Norse god of thunder.

Z = 90
Th
Thorium
Protactinium
Actinide

Protactinium is a rare, dense, silvery radioactive metal that sits between thorium and uranium in the periodic table and forms as an intermediate step in the natural decay chains of both. It has no significant commercial uses due to its scarcity, high radioactivity, and toxicity, and it exists mainly as a subject of nuclear physics research. Protactinium was one of the later actinides to be isolated in pure metallic form, in 1934.

Z = 91
Pa
Protactinium
Uranium
Actinide

Uranium is a dense, silvery-white radioactive metal best known as the primary fuel for nuclear power plants and the explosive core of the first atomic weapons. Naturally occurring uranium must be processed and enriched to increase the concentration of its fissile isotope, uranium-235, before it can sustain a nuclear chain reaction. Before its radioactivity was understood, uranium compounds were used to give ceramic glazes and glass a distinctive yellow-green color.

Z = 92
U
Uranium
Neptunium
Actinide

Neptunium was the first transuranium element to be discovered, synthesized in 1940 and named after the planet Neptune, following Uranium's naming after Uranus. It is a silvery radioactive metal produced in small quantities as a byproduct of nuclear reactor operation. Neptunium has few practical uses today beyond nuclear research and detectors for high-energy neutrons.

Z = 93
Np
Neptunium
Plutonium
Actinide

Plutonium is a silvery radioactive metal that tarnishes quickly in air and serves as a key fuel in both nuclear weapons and certain nuclear power reactors. It was first produced artificially in 1940 and played a central role in the Manhattan Project's 'Fat Man' bomb. Plutonium is also used to power radioisotope generators aboard deep-space probes that travel too far from the sun to rely on solar panels.

Z = 94
Pu
Plutonium
Americium
Actinide

Americium is a synthetic radioactive metal best known for its everyday presence in household smoke detectors, where a tiny amount ionizes air to detect smoke particles. It was first produced in 1944 during World War II-era nuclear research and named after the Americas, paralleling europium's naming after Europe. Outside of smoke detectors, americium has limited but important uses in industrial gauges and medical research.

Z = 95
Am
Americium
Curium
Actinide

Curium is a synthetic radioactive metal named after Marie and Pierre Curie in recognition of their pioneering research into radioactivity. It emits significant heat as it decays, making it useful as a power source for some spacecraft instruments, including devices used to analyze rocks on Mars. Curium is highly radioactive and toxic, requiring careful handling even in the small quantities typically produced.

Z = 96
Cm
Curium
Berkelium
Actinide

Berkelium is a synthetic radioactive metal first produced in 1949 at the University of California, Berkeley, after which it is named. It has no significant uses outside of scientific research, primarily serving as a target material for synthesizing even heavier superheavy elements in particle accelerators. Only small numbers of berkelium atoms have ever been produced, making it one of the most difficult elements to study directly.

Z = 97
Bk
Berkelium
Californium
Actinide

Californium is a synthetic radioactive metal named after the state and University of California, where it was first produced in 1950. It is a powerful source of neutrons, which makes it useful for starting up nuclear reactors, detecting metals and oil deposits, and treating certain cancers. Californium is extraordinarily expensive to produce, with costs historically estimated in the tens of millions of dollars per gram.

Z = 98
Cf
Californium
Einsteinium
Actinide

Einsteinium is a synthetic radioactive element first identified in the debris of the first hydrogen bomb test in 1952, though its discovery was kept secret for several years for security reasons. It is named after physicist Albert Einstein and has been produced only in extremely small quantities, mostly for basic scientific research. Einsteinium has no commercial or industrial applications due to its scarcity and intense radioactivity.

Z = 99
Es
Einsteinium
Fermium
Actinide

Fermium is a synthetic radioactive element also first detected in debris from the 1952 hydrogen bomb test, and it is named after physicist Enrico Fermi, a pioneer of nuclear reactor design. It has never been produced in large enough quantities to observe as a bulk material, existing only as individual atoms in specialized laboratories. Fermium's main significance today is as a benchmark in the study of heavy-element chemistry.

Z = 100
Fm
Fermium
Mendelevium
Actinide

Mendelevium is a synthetic radioactive element named after Dmitri Mendeleev, the chemist whose organization of the periodic table predicted the existence of many undiscovered elements. It was first synthesized in 1955 by bombarding einsteinium with helium ions, and only a handful of atoms have ever been produced at a time. Mendelevium has no practical applications beyond fundamental research into the properties of heavy elements.

Z = 101
Md
Mendelevium
Nobelium
Actinide

Nobelium is a synthetic radioactive element named after Alfred Nobel, the inventor of dynamite and founder of the Nobel Prizes. First convincingly synthesized in the early 1960s, it exists only fleetingly in particle accelerators and has no practical uses outside of nuclear physics research. Nobelium's chemical properties are difficult to study directly because so few atoms can be produced at once.

Z = 102
No
Nobelium
Lawrencium
Actinide

Lawrencium is a synthetic radioactive element and the final member of the actinide series, named after physicist Ernest O. Lawrence, inventor of the cyclotron particle accelerator. It was first synthesized in 1961 and, like many of the heaviest elements, has only ever been produced and studied one atom at a time. Lawrencium marks the boundary between the actinides and the transactinide elements that follow.

Z = 103
Lr
Lawrencium
Rutherfordium
Transition metal

Rutherfordium is a synthetic chemical element with atomic number 104, named after physicist Ernest Rutherford. As a synthetic element it is not found in nature and can only be made in a particle accelerator. It is radioactive; the most stable known isotope, 267Rf, has a half-life of about 48 minutes. It is a d-block element in period 7, the second of the fourth-row transition elements, and experiments have confirmed it behaves as the heavier homolog to hafnium in group 4.

Z = 104
Rf
Rutherfordium
Dubnium
Transition metal

Dubnium is a synthetic chemical element with atomic number 105. It is highly radioactive: the most stable known isotope, dubnium-268, has a half-life of about 16 hours, which greatly limits research on the element. It does not occur naturally and is produced artificially; it was formally named in 1997 after the town of Dubna, site of the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research. Theoretical research places dubnium in group 5 of the 6d series of transition metals, under vanadium, niobium, and tantalum.

Z = 105
Db
Dubnium
Seaborgium
Transition metal

Seaborgium is a synthetic chemical element with atomic number 106, named after American nuclear chemist Glenn T. Seaborg. As a synthetic element it can be created in a laboratory but is not found in nature, and it is radioactive, with the most stable known isotopes having half-lives on the order of only several minutes. It is a d-block transactinide element in period 7 and group 6, the fourth member of the 6d series of transition metals, and experiments have confirmed it behaves as the heavier homologue to tungsten.

Z = 106
Sg
Seaborgium
Bohrium
Transition metal

Bohrium is a synthetic chemical element with atomic number 107, named after Danish physicist Niels Bohr. It can only be created in particle accelerators and all known isotopes are highly radioactive; the most stable known isotope, 270Bh, has a half-life of about 2.4 minutes. It is a transactinide element in the d-block, a member of period 7 and group 7, and chemistry experiments have confirmed that bohrium behaves as the heavier homologue to rhenium.

Z = 107
Bh
Bohrium
Hassium
Transition metal

Hassium is a synthetic chemical element with atomic number 108. It is highly radioactive, with its most stable known isotopes having half-lives of about ten seconds, and it has been produced in a laboratory in very small quantities by fusing heavy nuclei with lighter ones. It is a transactinide element in period 7 and group 8, the sixth member of the 6d series of transition metals, and chemistry experiments have confirmed that hassium behaves as the heavier homologue to osmium, reacting readily with oxygen to form a volatile tetroxide.

Z = 108
Hs
Hassium
Meitnerium
Transition metal

Meitnerium is a synthetic chemical element with atomic number 109, an extremely radioactive element that is not naturally occurring but can be created in a laboratory. The most stable known isotope, meitnerium-278, has a half-life of 4.5 seconds. It was first synthesized in 1982 near Darmstadt, Germany, and named after Austrian-Swedish nuclear physicist Lise Meitner in 1997. It is a d-block transactinide element placed in group 9, calculated to have properties similar to its lighter homologues cobalt, rhodium, and iridium.

Z = 109
Mt
Meitnerium
Darmstadtium
Transition metal

Darmstadtium is a synthetic chemical element with atomic number 110. It is extremely radioactive: the most stable known isotope, darmstadtium-281, has a half-life of approximately 14 seconds. It was first created in 1994 at the GSI Helmholtz Centre for Heavy Ion Research in Darmstadt, Germany, after which it was named. It is a d-block transactinide element in period 7 and group 10, calculated to have properties similar to its lighter homologues nickel, palladium, and platinum.

Z = 110
Ds
Darmstadtium
Roentgenium
Transition metal

Roentgenium is a synthetic chemical element with atomic number 111. It is extremely radioactive and can only be created in a laboratory; the most stable known isotope, roentgenium-282, has a half-life of 130 seconds. It was first created in 1994 near Darmstadt, Germany, and is named after physicist Wilhelm Rontgen, who discovered X-rays. It is a d-block transactinide element in group 11, calculated to have properties similar to its lighter homologues copper, silver, and gold.

Z = 111
Rg
Roentgenium
Copernicium
Transition metal

Copernicium is a synthetic chemical element with atomic number 112. Its known isotopes are extremely radioactive and have only been created in a laboratory; the most stable known isotope, copernicium-285, has a half-life of approximately 30 seconds. It was first created in 1996 near Darmstadt, Germany, and named after astronomer Nicolaus Copernicus. During reactions with gold it has proven to be an extremely volatile element, so much so that it may be a gas or volatile liquid at standard temperature and pressure rather than a typical metal like its group 12 homologues zinc, cadmium, and mercury.

Z = 112
Cn
Copernicium
Nihonium
Post-transition metal

Nihonium is a synthetic chemical element with atomic number 113. It is extremely radioactive: its most stable known isotope, nihonium-286, has a half-life of about 10 seconds. It was first reported in 2003 by a Russian-American collaboration and independently in 2004 by Japanese scientists at Riken, who were ultimately credited with the discovery. The name comes from the common Japanese name for Japan, Nihon. It is a transactinide element in the p-block, a member of period 7 and group 13.

Z = 113
Nh
Nihonium
Flerovium
Post-transition metal

Flerovium is a synthetic chemical element with atomic number 114, an extremely radioactive superheavy element named after the Flerov Laboratory of Nuclear Reactions, where it was discovered in 1999, itself named after physicist Georgy Flyorov. It is a transactinide in the p-block of period 7, the heaviest known member of the carbon group. Its reaction with gold suggests it is very volatile and may even be gaseous at standard temperature and pressure, though it also shows some metallic properties consistent with being the heavier homologue of lead. Flerovium is predicted to sit near the center of the theorized island of stability.

Z = 114
Fl
Flerovium
Moscovium
Post-transition metal

Moscovium is a synthetic chemical element with atomic number 115, first synthesized in 2003 by a joint Russian-American team and officially named in 2016 after the Moscow region. It is extremely radioactive: its most stable known isotope, moscovium-290, has a half-life of only 0.65 seconds. It is a p-block transactinide element in period 7 and group 15, the heaviest pnictogen, calculated to have properties similar to its lighter homologues nitrogen, phosphorus, arsenic, antimony, and bismuth, as well as some similarities to thallium.

Z = 115
Mc
Moscovium
Livermorium
Post-transition metal

Livermorium is a synthetic chemical element with atomic number 116, an extremely radioactive element that has only been created in a laboratory and never observed in nature. It is named after the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, which collaborated with the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research to discover it between 2000 and 2006. Six isotopes are known, the longest-lived being livermorium-293 with a half-life of about 80 milliseconds. It is a p-block transactinide element in period 7 and group 16, the heaviest chalcogen, calculated to share some properties with oxygen, sulfur, selenium, tellurium, and polonium.

Z = 116
Lv
Livermorium
Tennessine
Halogen

Tennessine is a synthetic element with atomic number 117, named after the U.S. state of Tennessee, home to key research institutions involved in its discovery. Its discovery was officially announced in 2010 by a Russian-American collaboration, making it, at the time, the most recently discovered element. It may be located in the theorized island of stability, with synthesized atoms lasting tens to hundreds of milliseconds. In the periodic table it is expected to be a member of group 17, the halogens, though relativistic effects mean it is expected to behave as a volatile metal rather than a typical halogen.

Z = 117
Ts
Tennessine
Oganesson
Noble gas

Oganesson is a synthetic chemical element with atomic number 118, first synthesized in 2002 by a joint Russian-American team at the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research and formally named in 2016 after nuclear physicist Yuri Oganessian. It has the highest atomic number and atomic mass of all known elements. Its only known isotope, oganesson-294, is highly radioactive with a half-life of just 0.7 milliseconds, too short for chemical studies. Because of relativistic effects, theoretical studies predict it would actually be a solid and significantly reactive at room temperature, unlike the other noble gases in its group.

Z = 118
Og
Oganesson
